whoUses: everyone, all ages — one of the mildest, most flexible insults in Japanese
usageContext: Ranges from genuinely insulting (バカにするな, 'don't make fun of me') to affectionately teasing between close friends or couples (このバカ！, said fondly). Tone and relationship decide everything here — the word itself barely constrains it.
warningNotes: Softer than most English translations suggest, but still genuinely rude toward a stranger or superior — never use it upward (to a boss, teacher, elder).

examples.translation: What are you doing, you idiot!

whoUses: strongly associated with Kansai (Osaka/Kyoto) speech, where it's often gentler/more affectionate than バカ; can land harsher outside that region
usageContext: In Kansai, アホ is often the everyday, almost fond word for a goofball, while バカ can actually sound harsher there. In Tokyo/standard Japanese the reverse tends to hold — アホ can read as more of a genuine insult since it's marked as 'foreign' regional slang.
warningNotes: Register flips depending on region — this is exactly the kind of nuance worth a native Kansai speaker's confirmation before treating as settled.

examples.translation: What are you doing, you dummy. (Kansai-ben)

natural: damn / shit — as a standalone exclamation, or as an intensifying prefix meaning 'damn ___'
whoUses: informal speech, more common among men and younger speakers, though widely understood/used by everyone
usageContext: Standalone (クソ！'damn it!') or as a prefix that intensifies whatever follows: クソ暑い (kuso-atsui, 'damn hot'), クソゲー (kuso-gee, 'a terrible game'). Milder than English 'shit' in casual use, but still not polite company language.

literal: contraction of うっとうしい (uttoushii, 'annoying/oppressive')
usageContext: Said about a person, sound, or situation that's grating — a nagging parent, a buzzing fly, a repetitive ad. Blunt and dismissive; saying it to someone's face is a real, if mild, insult.

examples.text: その音、うざいんだけど。
examples.reading: そのおと、うざいんだけど。

literal: contraction of 気持ち悪い (kimochi warui, 'unpleasant feeling/gross')
usageContext: Can describe genuine physical disgust (bugs, spoiled food) or, much more cuttingly, describe a person as creepy/off-putting — the latter use is a real insult, not a mild complaint.
warningNotes: Said about a person rather than a thing, this is genuinely hurtful, not playful — closer to calling someone 'gross' to their face in English.

usageContext: About style, taste, or behavior that reads as try-hard or out of touch — an outfit, a ringtone, an overly earnest comment. Mild as insults go, but still a real jab about someone's choices.

examples.text: その服、ちょっとダサくない？
examples.reading: そのふく、ちょっとダサくない？
examples.translation: Isn't that outfit kind of lame?

whoUses: everyone informally, more common among men, all ages
usageContext: A frustration exclamation on losing, failing, or getting bad news — closer to 'dammit!' than any real insult aimed at a person, since it's usually directed at the situation, not someone specific.

examples.text: ちくしょう、間に合わなかった。
examples.reading: ちくしょう、まにあわなかった。
examples.translation: Dammit, I didn't make it in time.

literal: corrupted, rough form of 手前 (temae, 'in front of one's hand/oneself')
natural: a hostile, confrontational way of saying 'you'
whoUses: mostly men, in genuine confrontations or as a deliberately tough-guy fictional trope (very common in anime/manga/yakuza-genre media)
usageContext: One of the rudest ways to say 'you' in Japanese — pronouns already carry a lot of social weight, and this one signals open hostility or a direct challenge. Extremely common in fiction as a way to mark a character as aggressive; genuinely dangerous social territory to use for real.
warningNotes: This is fighting-words register in real life — recognize it in media, don't use it on an actual person unless you fully understand the confrontation you're inviting.

literal: negative imperative of ふざける (fuzakeru, 'to joke around/fool around')
natural: 'don't screw around!' / 'cut it out!' / 'don't mess with me!'
usageContext: A sharp, angry rebuke — said when someone's behavior (a lie, a insult, an unfair situation) has crossed a line. Casual clipped form ふざけんな is even more aggressive-sounding.

examples.text: ふざけるな、そんなの許さない。
examples.reading: ふざけるな、そんなのゆるさない。
examples.translation: Don't mess with me, I won't allow that.

literal: imperative of くたばる (kutabaru, a rough/vulgar verb for 'to die/kick the bucket')
whoUses: genuine anger, or as a dramatic fictional line; rarely said in earnest in daily life
usageContext: A real, severe curse — not casual venting. Common in fiction (villains, dramatic confrontations) but saying it to a real person is a serious escalation, not a throwaway insult.
warningNotes: Genuinely severe — treat as recognize-only-in-media, not usable vocabulary, the way you'd treat an equivalent severe curse in English.

examples.text: くたばれ、二度と来るな。
examples.translation: Drop dead, don't ever come back.

literal: imperative form of 死ぬ (shinu, 'to die')
natural: 'die' — one of the most severe things you can say to someone in Japanese
whoUses: extreme anger, online harassment/flaming, or deliberately shocking fiction — essentially never appropriate in genuine daily life
usageContext: Japanese generally has fewer, less systematized insults than languages like English or Portuguese, precisely because the social weight normally carried by profanity gets carried by register/politeness violations instead — 死ね is one of the rare exceptions that's severe by literal content, not just tone. It shows up in online flame wars and as a shock line in fiction.
warningNotes: As severe as language gets in Japanese — recognize it (especially if you encounter it in online spaces or media) but there is no context in ordinary life where a learner should say this.

examples.text: （オンライン上の暴言の例）死ねよ。
examples.reading: （オンラインじょうのぼうげんのれい）しねよ。
examples.romaji: (Onrain-jou no bougen no rei) Shine yo.

literal: 殺す (korosu, 'to kill') intensified with the rough prefix ぶっ
natural: 'I'll kill you' — almost always hyperbolic/fictional rather than a literal threat
whoUses: fiction (action/yakuza-genre media), heated arguments as pure hyperbole, gaming trash talk
usageContext: Functions like English 'I'll kill you' does between friends over something trivial — almost always understood as hyperbole, not a genuine threat, but the literal content is severe and it reads very differently in a real confrontation versus a joking one between people who know each other.
warningNotes: Extremely context-dependent — the exact same words are harmless joking hyperbole between close friends and a genuinely alarming threat in any other context. Not worth the risk for a learner to produce.

examples.text: （フィクションの決め台詞）てめえ、ぶっ殺す！
examples.translation: (A dramatic fiction line) You — I'll kill you!
